lens.included_activities: US firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced human-resource policy, benefits, compensation, wage-administration, and personnel-management advice or administration to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: One-time-only engagements, captive internal HR units, employment-placement agencies, executive-search firms, training providers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External business customers; recurring retainers, administration fees, repeat advisory assignments, and fixed-fee or time-based consulting engagements.

a.rationale: The task evidence supports meaningful exposure in drafting, records, data processing, policy research, reporting, and preliminary screening, while client counseling, conflict resolution, judgment on employment law, and accountable recommendations remain less substitutable. The wage-weighted estimate treats the latter work as a material share of consulting labor rather than assuming all computer-based work is replaceable. S2 shows business and financial operations are 33.12% of the broader consulting workforce and S3 documents both administrative and interpersonal HR work.
a.change_evidence: A firm-level time-and-payroll study that maps billed hours by task, role, wage, and AI-assisted workflow; client-approved productivity measurements for HR policy, benefits, and compensation engagements; and legal-review rework rates.
caveats: There is no six-digit wage-by-task dataset for the frozen lens.
S2 covers the broader NAICS 541600 industry and excludes self-employed owners.
S3 describes HR specialists across employers, not HR consulting firms, and includes recruiting tasks outside the lens.

rho.rationale: Drafting, knowledge retrieval, document comparison, workflow triage, and structured analysis can be introduced with review, but client data access, system integration, validation, and employment-law risk slow operational implementation. S6 establishes that employment uses of software and AI require ADA-related consideration, and S7 describes governance, mapping, measurement, and management activities for AI risk.
rho.change_evidence: Audited adoption data from HR consultancies showing task-level before-and-after hours, human-review rates, client security approvals, and production error or adverse-impact findings.
caveats: The sources describe risk-management needs and legal guidance, not implementation rates for this industry.
Implementation will vary substantially by client data access, service line, and the operator's review controls.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition includes HR policies, benefits planning and administration, compensation systems, and wage administration. Those activities can support repeat outsourced service, but the code also contains project-oriented advisory work and firms too dependent on a principal or a single engagement to meet the lens. The estimate therefore retains a majority, not all, of the injected LMM count.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit establishment file matched to revenue concentration, contract cadence, owner dependence, EBITDA, and service-line descriptions; broker confidential-information memoranda; and customer-contract samples.
caveats: No public data directly measures recurring external revenue or transferability for six-digit LMM firms.
The injected firm count spans the full NAICS code and can include work outside the recurring-service lens.

s5.rationale: Gallup found that 22% of employer-business owners reported plans to sell or transfer ownership within five years. The base is below that intended-transition figure because a qualifying control transfer requires a transferable operating business and excludes closures, internal reorganizations, and nontransferable owner-dependent practices.
s5.change_evidence: Industry-specific closed-deal data from business brokers and transaction databases, together with owner-age, succession-plan, EBITDA, customer-concentration, and seller-discretionary-earnings records for firms in the lens.
caveats: S4 is a survey of US business owners generally, rather than NAICS 541612 LMM firms.
Reported intent to sell or transfer is not an observed completed control transfer.

q.rationale: Repeat advisory and administration relationships can retain part of the benefit through capacity, speed, and quality, especially where the provider bears workflow risk. However, hourly and project consulting fees expose savings to utilization reductions, client procurement, renewal repricing, and competing AI-enabled providers. S1 confirms that the industry is principally advice and assistance, rather than a regulated utility with a protected tariff.
q.change_evidence: Executed master-service agreements, statements of work, renewal notices, rate cards, utilization reports, and client interviews that identify fixed-fee, time-based, gain-share, and cost-pass-through terms.
caveats: Public sources do not provide contract economics for the frozen lens.
Retention can differ sharply between administration retainers and bespoke executive advisory work.

d5.bridge: S5 measures US employment of human resources specialists across all industries, rising from 944.3 thousand in 2024 to 1,002.7 thousand in 2034. I convert its ten-year 6.2% change to an approximate five-year demand anchor for the current HR-consulting service basket, then widen the range for substitution between in-house and outsourced work. This can overstate demand because growth in in-house specialist employment need not create outsourcing demand, and can understate demand if regulatory or benefits complexity causes customers to outsource more work.
d5.rationale: The proxy indicates modest growth in the adjacent HR work base, so the central demand case is modestly above current quantity rather than dependent on price inflation. The low case allows client budget pressure and self-service to offset this growth; the high case allows additional outsourcing of specialized HR work.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price industry revenue and volume series for HR consulting; client procurement spend by service line; renewal and retention cohorts; and a survey separating outsourced HR, benefits, compensation, and wage-administration demand from in-house staffing.
caveats: Occupation employment is not a direct measure of demand for outsourced HR consulting.
The projection horizon is longer than the five-year horizon and the source is national rather than lens-specific.

o.rationale: A substantial portion should still require an accountable provider for client-specific policy interpretation, sensitive employee matters, compliance assessment, benefits and compensation design, and final recommendations. S3 shows HR work includes interpersonal relationships, judgment, compliance evaluation, conflict resolution, and advice alongside recordkeeping and information processing; the latter tasks are more vulnerable to self-service or software-only supply.
o.change_evidence: Client workflow studies identifying which engagements are replaced by software, self-service, or retained operator review; product adoption and churn data from HR software vendors; and post-implementation interviews with buyers who ended or reduced consulting contracts.
caveats: This is a judgment about accountable-service need, not a forecast of headcount.
The surviving share may be lower for standardized administrative work and higher for regulated, conflict-sensitive, or executive advisory engagements.

narrative.executive_view: Human Resources Consulting Services contains repeat outsourced work in policies, benefits, compensation, and wage administration. AI can reduce effort in document-heavy and structured workflows, but the industry also depends on contextual advice, client trust, and accountable judgment in sensitive employment matters.
narrative.ai_impact: The clearest workflow changes are research, drafting, records, reporting, intake, screening support, and comparison of policy or compensation materials. O*NET also identifies client communication, relationship building, compliance evaluation, conflict resolution, and advice, which limits full task substitution and calls for human review.
narrative.value_capture: Value capture depends on contract form. Retainers and fixed-fee administration can preserve capacity and quality gains, while time-based and project fees make lower hours visible to customers and competitors. Contract review is necessary before treating labor savings as retained economics.
narrative.firm_availability: The relevant supply is narrower than the NAICS count because the lens excludes one-off, owner-dependent, or nonrecurring practices. Gallup's employer-owner survey supplies a general transition-intent anchor, but closed transfers for this industry and EBITDA band need direct broker or transaction evidence.
narrative.demand_durability: The base demand view is modest real growth, using HR-specialist employment projections only as an imperfect anchor. Routine administration can migrate to self-service software, but clients continue to need accountable assistance for policy, benefits, compensation, and employment-risk decisions.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The major uncertainties are service-line mix, recurring revenue, owner dependence, billing terms, client data permissions, and legal review requirements. Employment AI introduces disability-discrimination and broader trustworthiness considerations, so a fast deployment assumption would be poorly supported.
narrative.principal_driver: Document-intensive HR workflows offer operational leverage when paired with accountable expert review.
narrative.principal_weakness: Public evidence does not isolate recurring LMM HR-consulting firms or their contract economics from the broader NAICS population.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition for 541612 Human Resources Consulting Services
sources.supports: Defines the industry as advice and assistance in HR policies, benefits planning, compensation systems, and wage and salary administration.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates, NAICS 541600
sources.supports: Reports that business and financial operations occupations were 33.12% of employment in NAICS 541600 and human resources specialists were 42,450 workers, or 2.30%.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Human Resources Specialists
sources.supports: Lists HR specialist tasks and activities including policy interpretation, record maintenance, benefits administration, compliance evaluation, information processing, interpersonal relationships, conflict resolution, and advising.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years in a fall 2024 survey.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Projections and Worker Characteristics, 2024 to 2034
sources.supports: Projects human resources specialists from 944.3 thousand workers in 2024 to 1,002.7 thousand in 2034, a 6.2% increase.

sources.title: EEOC: Artificial Intelligence and the ADA
sources.supports: Links EEOC technical assistance on applying the Americans with Disabilities Act to software, algorithms, and AI used to assess job applicants and employees.

sources.title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework Core
sources.supports: Describes governance, mapping, measurement, management, and human-AI oversight practices for managing AI risks.
